stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[b]])x^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]]) \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: The slope of the tangent line is \( f'([[x0]]) \). Since \( f'(x) = 3([[a]])x^{2} + 2([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \), evaluating at \( x = [[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This evaluates \( f([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its slope.
This is \( f''([[x0]]) \), the second derivative.
This drops the factor of 2 on the \( 2([[b]])x \) term of \( f' \).
This is off by one from the correct slope.
This is off by three from the correct slope.
This is off by three from the correct slope.

stem: Let \( f(x) = ([[a]]x + ([[b]]))([[c]]x + ([[d]])) \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: By the product rule, \( f'(x) = [[a]]([[c]]x+([[d]])) + [[c]]([[a]]x+([[b]])) \). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This keeps only the first term of the product rule, dropping the second factor's contribution.
This keeps only the second term of the product rule, dropping the first factor's contribution.
This multiplies only the leading coefficients instead of applying the product rule.
This evaluates \( f([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its slope.
This is off by two from the correct slope.
This is off by two from the correct slope.

stem: Let \( f(x) = ([[a]]x + ([[b]]))^{[[n]]} \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( f'(x) = [[n]]([[a]]x+([[b]]))^{[[n]]-1}\cdot [[a]] \). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the chain-rule factor of \( [[a]] \) from the outer derivative.
This forgets to lower the exponent by one.
This treats \( f \) as if it were linear, reporting only the inner coefficient.
This evaluates \( f([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its slope.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct slope.
This is off by \( [[n]] \) from the correct slope.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]x + ([[b]])}{[[c]]x + ([[d]])} \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = [[x0]] \)?

This adds instead of subtracts in the quotient-rule numerator.

stem: Let \( f(x) = \sqrt{[[a]]x + ([[b]])} \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( f'(x) = \dfrac{[[a]]}{2\sqrt{[[a]]x+([[b]])}} \). Since \( [[a]]\cdot[[x0]]+([[b]]) = [[k]]^{2} \), evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 in the denominator from the chain rule.
This ignores the inner expression entirely.
This evaluates \( f([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its slope.
This squares the numerator instead of applying the chain rule correctly.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]e^{[[b]]x} + ([[c]]) \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = 0 \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( f'(x) = [[a]]\cdot[[b]]\cdot e^{[[b]]x} \). Since \( e^{0}=1 \), evaluating at \( x=0 \) gives \( f'(0) = [[a]]\cdot[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the chain-rule factor of \( [[b]] \).
This evaluates \( f(0) \), the function value, not its slope.
This has the wrong sign.
This mistakenly subtracts the constant term, which does not affect the derivative.
This is off by two from the correct slope.
This is off by two from the correct slope.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]\ln([[b]]x) \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = [[x0]] \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( f'(x) = [[a]]\cdot\dfrac{[[b]]}{[[b]]x} = \dfrac{[[a]]}{x} \) (the factor of \( [[b]] \) cancels). Evaluating at \( x=[[x0]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets that the factor of \( [[b]] \) cancels in \( \frac{[[b]]}{[[b]]x} \).
This mistakenly treats the derivative of \( \ln([[b]]x) \) as \( \frac{1}{[[b]]} \).
This evaluates \( f([[x0]]) \), the function value, not its slope.
This has the wrong sign.
This is off by one from the correct slope.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]\sin([[b]]x) + ([[c]]) \). What is the slope of the tangent line to the curve \( y = f(x) \) at the point where \( x = 0 \)?
solution: By the chain rule, \( f'(x) = [[a]]\cdot[[b]]\cdot\cos([[b]]x) \). Since \( \cos(0)=1 \), evaluating at \( x=0 \) gives \( f'(0) = [[a]]\cdot[[b]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the chain-rule factor of \( [[b]] \).
This evaluates \( f(0) \), the function value, not its slope.
This has the wrong sign.
This squares the chain-rule factor instead of using it once.
This is off by two from the correct slope.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The tangent line to the curve \( y=f(x) \) at the point where \( x=[[x0]] \) has equation \( y = mx + k \). What is \( k \), the \( y \)-intercept of this tangent line?
solution: The tangent line is \( y = f([[x0]]) + f'([[x0]])(x-[[x0]]) \). Its \( y \)-intercept is \( f([[x0]]) - f'([[x0]])\cdot[[x0]] \), which simplifies to \( [[c]] - [[a]]\cdot[[x0]]^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This reports \( f([[x0]]) \) itself, not the tangent line's \( y \)-intercept.
This has the wrong sign on the \( a x_0^2 \) correction term.
This drops the correction term entirely, leaving just the constant of \( f \).
This is the slope \( f'([[x0]]) \), not the \( y \)-intercept.
This is off by two from the correct \( y \)-intercept.
This is off by two from the correct \( y \)-intercept.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + ([[b]])x + ([[c]]) \). The tangent line to the curve \( y=f(x) \) at the point where \( x=[[x0]] \) crosses the \( x \)-axis at \( x = t \). What is \( t \)?
solution: The tangent line is \( y = f([[x0]]) + f'([[x0]])(x-[[x0]]) \). Setting \( y=0 \) and solving for \( x \) gives \( t = [[x0]] - \dfrac{f([[x0]])}{f'([[x0]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the correction term and just reports \( x_0 \) itself.
This adds instead of subtracts the correction term.
This inverts the numerator and denominator of the correction term.
This forgets to divide by the slope \( f'([[x0]]) \).
This is off by one from the correct \( x \)-intercept.
This is off by one from the correct \( x \)-intercept.
